coredata.dc:title: A pH-dependent N, P co-doped carbon dots as fluorescent probe for malachite green assay and its visual application based on fluorescent hydrogel kit
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Ning
Song, Jianqiang
Ye, Heng
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: A pH-dependent red fluorescent N, P co-doped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) was firstly developed for rapid, sensitive and visual detection of the toxic dye malachite green (MG). The synthesis of N, P-CDs was carried out by hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid, and the low cytotoxicity of N, P-CDs was verified by Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) method. The linear decrease of N, P-CDs fluorescence in the pH range of 3.8–5.6 was attributed to the reduction of protonation of nitrogen-containing groups on the surface. And the sensing mechanism for MG was constructed based on the inner filter effect (IFE). The obtained linear ranges were 0.08–1 and 1–50 µmol·L−1 with limit of detections (LODs) of 0.07 and 0.74 µmol·L−1. The proposed method has been successfully applied to the determination of MG in water and fish samples. In addition, a kind of N, P-CDs fluorescent hydrogel kit was prepared for the visual analysis of MG. This method is not only simple, rapid and accurate, but also the prepared fluorescent hydrogel has potential application prospects for on-site qualitative identification and semi-quantitative analysis of MG, which also provides a new idea for monitoring MG in water environment and fish products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, P co-doped carbon dots
pH-dependent
Malachite green
Inner filter effect
Visualization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of MG in real samples by N, P-CDs
Determination of MG in real samples by HPLC
Preparation of N, P-CDs fluorescent hydrogel kits
Synthesis and characterization of N, P-CDs
Quenching mechanisms of N, P-CDs by pH and MG
Sensing performance of N, P-CDs for pH
Fluorescence detection of MG by N, P-CDs
Selectivity of N, P-CDs for MG determination
Analysis of MG in real samples
Visual analysis of MG by N, P-CDs fluorescent hydrogel kit

Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University ( ZQN2016011 ), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department ( 2020LZD02 ), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province ( 2020-181 ), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the N, P-CDs (Inset show the HRTEM image), (B) Size distribution of N, P-CDs, (C) FT-IR spectrum of N, P-CDs.
(A) The full XPS spectrum of N, P-CDs and high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (B), N 1 s (C), O 1 s (D) and P 2p (E).

(A) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of N, P-CDs (Inset: images of the N, P-CDs under natural light and 365 nm UV light), (B) Emission spectra of N, P-CDs at various excitation wavelengths, the effects of (C) radiation time, (D) NaCl concentration and (E) storage time on the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs.

(A) Schematic representation of the surface states of N, P-CDs under acidic conditions. (B) The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N, P-CDs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MG, (C) The fluorescence lifetime decay spectra of N, P-CDs before and after the addition of MG.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of N, P-CDs at different pH values (3.8–5.6), (B) Linear regression equation for pH determination.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N, P-CDs at various concentrations of MG, (B) and (C) Linear calibration curves of MG in the ranges of 0.08–1 and 1–50 µmol·L−1, (D) Selectivity and anti-interference of N, P-CDs for MG detection.
(A) Fluorescence color changes of N, P-CDs fluorescent hydrogels with increasing MG concentrations under the 365 nm UV light, (B) Selectivity of N, P-CDs fluorescent hydrogels for MG.
Schematic illustration of this analytical strategy based on the N, P-CDs for pH and MG detection.

head.ce:title: A pH-dependent N, P co-doped carbon dots as fluorescent probe for malachite green assay and its visual application based on fluorescent hydrogel kit

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning 110016, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110016, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning 110016, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A pH-dependent red fluorescent N, P co-doped carbon dots (N, P-CDs) was firstly developed for rapid, sensitive and visual detection of the toxic dye malachite green (MG). The synthesis of N, P-CDs was carried out by hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid, and the low cytotoxicity of N, P-CDs was verified by Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) method. The linear decrease of N, P-CDs fluorescence in the pH range of 3.8–5.6 was attributed to the reduction of protonation of nitrogen-containing groups on the surface. And the sensing mechanism for MG was constructed based on the inner filter effect (IFE). The obtained linear ranges were 0.08–1 and 1–50 µmol·L−1 with limit of detections (LODs) of 0.07 and 0.74 µmol·L−1. The proposed method has been successfully applied to the determination of MG in water and fish samples. In addition, a kind of N, P-CDs fluorescent hydrogel kit was prepared for the visual analysis of MG. This method is not only simple, rapid and accurate, but also the prepared fluorescent hydrogel has potential application prospects for on-site qualitative identification and semi-quantitative analysis of MG, which also provides a new idea for monitoring MG in water environment and fish products.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of this analytical strategy based on the N, P-CDs for pH and MG detection

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Malachite green (MG), as a triphenylmethane dye, is not only extensively used in leather, silk and paper industries [1,2], but also used as a fungicide and insecticide in fish culture owing to its effective antibacterial activity [3]. However, studies have shown that MG also exhibits extremely high toxicity, and may pose potential carcinogenic and teratogenic risks on animals and humans [4,5]. Therefore, the use of MG in fish culture has been explicitly prohibited in many countries [6]. But the illegal use of MG frequently exists because of the properties of potent antibacterial activity and low cost [7]. Consequently, developing a simple, rapid and reliable method for the determination of MG is of high demand to ensure the environment, food safety and human health.

Over the past several years, various analytical strategies have been established for the sensitive detection of MG, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8,9], electrochemistry [10], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [11], immunoassay [12,13] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [14,15]. Although these techniques provide good selectivity, high sensitivity and accuracy, these strategies are not suitable for rapid on-site analysis of MG on account of time-consuming, expensive instruments and cumbersome pre-treatment process. Nevertheless, the fluorescence methods, particularly the fluorescent probes, have been gradually developed owing to the advantages of rapidity, low cost and high sensitivity. Recently, Hu et al. [16] reported a sensitive and rapid method for the determination of MG using Se, N, Cl-doped carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent probe via inner filter effect (IFE). At present, the fluorescence detection of MG is rarely studied, and there is a large space for further exploration and development.

CDs, an emerging fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, have attracted extensive research interest in recent years. Studies have shown that CDs possess unique physicochemical and optical properties, such as excellent photostability, tunable photoluminescence, good water solubility and biocompatibility [17–19], which make CDs have broad application prospects in the fields of sensing [20,21], bioimaging [22], drug delivery [23–25], catalysis [26,27] and antibacterial [28]. Currently, the synthesized CDs mainly exhibit blue and green photoluminescence, which are easily interfered by tissue autofluorescence in some fields, such as bioimaging [29]. In view of the importance of red fluorescence in bioimaging and biosensing, it is imperative to prepare CDs with red fluorescence and long-wavelength emission. Doping, as an efficient way to adjust the emission wavelength of CDs for various sensor monitoring, has attracted extensive attention [30]. Moreover, doping is also an effective strategy to improve the structural performance of CDs, which can enhance the quantum yield and improve the optical and chemical properties of CDs [31,32]. Phosphorus, as n-type donor, can alter the photophysical properties of CDs by creating substitutional defects, thereby generating new energy levels and inducing unanticipated fluorescence properties [33,34]. For example, Li et al. [30] prepared a kind of phosphorus-doped CDs whose blue fluorescence red shifted to orange-yellow with the increase of solution concentration, that is, the process of aggregation induced red shifted emission occurred. This phenomenon indicates that phosphorus doping can form an electron-rich system on the surface of CDs and then cause a red shift of emission wavelength. More importantly, visualization analysis based on the photoluminescence of CDs is an important feature of CDs in sensing applications. At present, a kind of portable kit for on-site detection has been gradually developed. Among them, agarose hydrogel kits have been widely used for visual analysis due to the superiorities of low cost, simple preparation, no background fluorescence and large carrying capacity [35].

In this study, a pH-dependent and low-toxicity red fluorescent N, P-CDs was prepared by hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid, and a simple and rapid sensing strategy for pH and MG determination was established ( Scheme 1). Owing to the reduction of protonation, N, P-CDs exhibited pH-dependent property in the range of 3.8–5.6. The fluorescence of N, P-CDs could be selectively quenched by MG through the inner filter effect (IFE). Based on the above mechanism, a kind of N, P-CDs fluorescent probe was developed for MG detection in aquaculture water, river water, lake water, carp and grass carp. In addition, a fluorescent hydrogel kit based on the property of N, P-CDs was prepared for visual analysis of MG. Thus, this proposed method achieved rapid, sensitive and accurate determination of MG, which had broad application prospects in the fields of environmental monitoring and food safety.
This section was described in detail in the supporting information.
This section was described in the supporting information.
The synthesis of N, P-CDs was carried out by the previously reported method with slight modification [36]. The synthesis process was described in the supporting information.

The pH measurements were carried out in 0.2 mol·L−1 HAc-NaAc buffer solutions at different pH values. Briefly, 80 µL of HAc-NaAc buffer solutions at various pH values were added to 50 µL of N, P-CDs (40 µg·mL−1) solution. And the mixed solutions were diluted to 1 mL with ultrapure water. Finally, the fluorescence emission spectra at the excitation wavelength of 611 nm were recorded. Each sample was prepared in triplicate.

In a typical MG assay, 400 µL of MG solutions of various concentrations (0–50 µmol·L−1) were mixed with 50 µL of N, P-CDs (20 µg·mL−1). Next, the mixtures were diluted to 1 mL with ultrapure water, and the pH of the solutions was adjusted to 3 with HCl/NaOH (0.1 mol·L−1). After 20 s of violent vortex at room temperature, the emission spectra at the excitation wavelength of 611 nm were measured. Each sample was prepared in triplicate.

The limit of detection (LOD) for the determination of MG by N, P-CDs was calculated according to the 3σ/k rule, where σ represented the standard deviation of eleven blank samples and k was the slope of linear regression equation.

Furthermore, the selectivity of this method for the determination of MG was investigated by recording the fluorescence responses of N, P-CDs towards some potential interfering substances including dyes, metal ions and anions. Similarly, the anti-interference ability of this method for MG detection was evaluated by recording the fluorescence responses of the N, P-CDs/MG system to the above interferences. Each sample was prepared in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MG in real samples by N, P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The feasibility of this proposed method was verified by measuring the concentration of MG in local environmental water samples, and fish samples purchased from local supermarket. Firstly, three kinds of water samples, aquaculture water, river water and lake water, were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Then, the supernatant was collected and filtered with 0.22 µm filter membrane to obtain pretreated water samples. Subsequently, carp and grass carp samples were pretreated according to the reported method with slight modification [37]. 1 g of minced fish tissue and 5 mL of acetonitrile were added into a 10 mL centrifuge tube and sonicated for 10 min. After centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane to obtain pretreated fish samples. Finally, 400 µL of pretreated samples were added to 50 µL of N, P-CDs (20 µg·mL−1) solutions, respectively, and the contents of MG were measured by the above method. Besides, different concentrations of MG were spiked into water and fish samples, and the recoveries were calculated based on the following equation to assess the accuracy of this method. Each sample was repeated three times.
Recovery ( % ) =
C
a
-
C
s
C
M
× 100 %
Where C
a represents the sample concentration after the addition of MG, C
s is the sample concentration and C
M is the MG concentration added.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MG in real samples by HPLC
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify the assay results, the contents of MG in water and fish samples were also measured by HPLC on the basis of the previous work with minor modification [4]. The method was described in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N, P-CDs fluorescent hydrogel kits
ce:section.ce:para: 0.2 g of agarose was added to 20 mL of ultrapure water and heated continuously until the agarose was fully dissolved. Then, 1 mL of N, P-CDs (100 µg·mL−1) was added and mixed thoroughly with the above solution. And then, 200 µL of the above mixed solution was quickly transferred into the vial. As the mixture completely solidified, the N, P-CDs fluorescent hydrogel kit was formed. Finally, 400 µL of MG solutions with various concentrations and some potential interfering substances were added to the vials, respectively. After 30 min, the fluorescence changes of the hydrogel were recorded under 365 nm UV lamp.
This section was described in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N, P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, some factors, such as the volume of H3PO4, reaction temperature and time, which could significantly affect the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs, were investigated. Firstly, we studied the effects of different volumes of H3PO4 that were controlled at 0.25, 0.375, 0.5, 0.75 and 1 mL, respectively. As presented in Fig. S1A, N, P-CDs exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity at the volume of 0.5 mL. After that, the influences of reaction temperature and time were optimized. It could be clearly seen from Fig. S1B that the reaction temperature of 180 °C corresponded to the strongest fluorescence of N, P-CDs, which meant that 180 ℃ was the most suitable synthesis temperature. Fig. S1C displayed that the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs reached the maximum at 10 h. Thus, 0.5 mL of H3PO4, 180 °C and 10 h were determined as the optimal synthesis conditions for N, P-CDs. Besides, we also prepared CDs without phosphoric acid under the reaction conditions of 180 °C and 10 h. As shown in Fig. S2, the CDs exhibited obvious yellow fluorescence, which was completely different from the red fluorescence of N, P-CDs. This further indicated that the doping of phosphorus caused the red shift of the emission wavelength of N, P-CDs.

Subsequently, the synthesized N, P-CDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR and XPS. First, the morphology and size of N, P-CDs were investigated by TEM. As displayed in Fig. 1 A, N, P-CDs exhibited excellent dispersion. Fig. 1B depicted the size distribution of N, P-CDs ranging from 1.05 to 4.98 nm with an average size of 3.02 nm. And there was an apparent lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Inset of Fig. 1 A), corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite [38].

After that, the surface structure of N, P-CDs was confirmed by FT-IR technique, and the results were shown in Fig. 1 C. The characteristic absorption peaks at 2922, 1649 and 1401 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of O-H, CO and N-H bonds [39]. The absorption peak located at 2389 cm−1 was related to the OCO stretching vibration [40]. And the peaks at 1120, 994 and 493 cm−1 were assigned to the PO, P-O-R and PO4 3- bonds, respectively [36].

The elemental composition of N, P-CDs was further analyzed by XPS spectrum. As presented in Fig. 2 A, the full XPS spectrum showed that N, P-CDs were mainly composed of C, N, O and P elements with relative contents of 29.3%, 2.11%, 54.54% and 14.05%, respectively. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 2B) indicated that the characteristic peaks at 284.4, 285.2, 286.1 and 289 eV were successively attributed to CC, C-N/C-P, C-O and O-CO bonds [41,42]. In the spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 2 C), two peaks at 399.8 and 401.8 eV were associated with N-H and C-N/P-N groups [42]. Fig. 2D revealed that the three peaks at 531.7, 532.9 and 533.6 eV in the O 1 s spectrum were ascribed to CO, C-O and C-OH groups [39], respectively. The spectrum of P 2p (Fig. 2E) exhibited two peaks at 133 and 133.9 eV, illustrating the presence of P-O and P-C/P-N bonds [36]. In brief, these results were consistent with the FT-IR data, confirming the successful synthesis of N, P-CDs.

Moreover, the optical properties of N, P-CDs were further investigated by the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. As represented in Fig. 3 A, two strong absorption peaks appeared at 234 and 278 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N, P-CDs, which were related to the π-π * transition of CC and the n-π * transition induced by N, O and P heteroatoms, respectively. And the broad absorption peak in the range of 350–550 nm might be attributed to the amino or phosphate groups on the surface of N, P-CDs [43]. Fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 3 A) indicated that N, P-CDs presented the maximum emission peak at 624 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength of 611 nm. Meanwhile, N, P-CDs emitted bright red fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 3 A). Fig. 3B illustrated that N, P-CDs showed excitation-independent property at different excitation wavelengths. And the absolute quantum yield of N, P-CDs was calculated to be 17.28%.

And the effects of radiation time, ion intensity and storage time on the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs were also investigated in this study. As shown in Fig. 3C-E, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs manifested negligible changes under continuous radiation at 611 nm excitation for 100 min, high ionic strength (1 mol·L−1, NaCl) and storage within 40 days. These results demonstrated that the prepared N, P-CDs possessed excellent stability, which provided a guarantee for the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanisms of N, P-CDs by pH and MG
ce:section.ce:para: The decreased fluorescence of N, P-CDs in the pH range of 3.8–5.6 was due to the reduced protonation of nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of N, P-CDs. Under acidic conditions, the nitrogenous groups on the surface of N, P-CDs were prone to protonation, which meant that the lone pair electrons on the nitrogen atoms coordinated with hydrogen ions, resulting in surface passivation. With pH increasing, the emission intensity of the nanoparticles almost quenched to zero. On the one hand, the protonated nitrogenous groups decreased, thereby increasing the surface defects of N, P-CDs ( Fig. 4 A). Meanwhile, this change increased the non-radiative transition of N, P-CDs, which in turn decreased the fluorescence intensity [44,45]. On the other hand, the emission peak position (Fig. S3) showed a slight red shift which might be due to the aggregation of nanoparticles by forming hydrogen bonds through hydroxyl ions [44]. These assumptions were further supported by zeta potential and DLS size distribution analysis (Table S1).

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of N, P-CDs by MG was also explored by UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectrum and fluorescence lifetime. We first discussed whether there was an overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MG and the fluorescence spectra of N, P-CDs. As presented in Fig. 4B, the absorption spectrum of MG overlapped greatly with the excitation and emission spectra of N, P-CDs. This suggested that the fluorescence quenching induced by MG might be associated with IFE or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [46,47]. Subsequently, the fluorescence lifetimes of N, P-CDs in the presence and absence of MG were measured. Fig. 4 C showed that the average lifetime of N, P-CDs changed slightly from 1.75 ns to 1.73 ns after the addition of MG. This negligible change eliminated the possibility of FRET whose average fluorescence lifetime significantly reduced. Therefore, the above results indicated that IFE was the mechanism by which MG quenched the fluorescence of N, P-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of N, P-CDs for pH
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a sensing platform for pH determination was established based on the pH-dependent property of N, P-CDs. As could be seen from Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs gradually decreased with the increase of pH value. And Fig. 5B displayed a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs and pH values ranging from 3.8 to 5.6, with a linear equation of y = −1313.8x + 7433.5 (R2 = 0.9944).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of MG by N, P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the best analytical performance for the detection of MG, some influencing factors such as the concentration of N, P-CDs, pH and reaction time were optimized in this experiment. Primarily, the concentration of N, P-CDs that could significantly affect the sensitivity and linear range of MG assay was investigated. As represented in Fig. S4A, the F0/F (F0 and F denoted the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs before and after the addition of MG) reached the maximum when the concentration was 20 µg·mL−1. Posteriorly, the optimal pH and reaction time were also explored. Fig. S4B illustrated that the F0/F reached the optimum at pH 3. And MG was able to quench the fluorescence of N, P-CDs completely after 20 s (Fig. S4C), indicating that this method could achieve the rapid detection of MG. Hence, 20 µg·mL−1 of N, P-CDs, pH 3, 20 s were used for the following experiments.

Under optimal conditions, this study verified the feasibility of N, P-CDs for the determination of MG. As displayed in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs decreased gradually with the increase of MG concentration. Fig. 6B-C exhibited that the F0/F varied linearly with MG concentrations in the ranges of 0.08–1 and 1–50 µmol·L−1. And the corresponding regression equations were y = 0.0947x + 1.0214 (R2 = 0.9955) and y = 0.0424x + 1.0257 (R2 = 0.9966), respectively, where y and x represented F0/F and MG concentration. The relevant LODs calculated according to the 3σ/k rule were 0.07 and 0.74 µmol·L−1, respectively. Besides, compared with other methods in Table S2, this method is able to achieve more rapid, sensitive and accurate detection of MG. Importantly, the synthesized N, P-CDs also possess superiorities such as low cost, simple synthesis, reliability, and low toxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of N, P-CDs for MG determination
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the proposed method for detecting MG were evaluated by analyzing the effects of some common dyes, metal ions and anions on the fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 6D, it was found that MG significantly quenched the fluorescence of N, P-CDs, while these dyes (CV, MO, CR), metal ions (Ca2+, Na+, Ba2+, K+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Ag+, Mg2+) and anions (SO4 2-, HCO3 -, CO3 2-) exhibited negligible influence on N, P-CDs fluorescence. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching of N, P-CDs by MG was also hardly affected by the coexistence of MG with these potential interferers. Therefore, this study showed excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability for MG determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of MG in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the applicability of this method, MG concentrations in three kinds of water samples (aquaculture water, river water, lake water) and two kinds of fish samples (carp, grass carp) were measured. It was found that no MG was detected in these actual samples. To further prove the accuracy of the method, the standard addition method was carried out and the HPLC was used for comparison. The results in Table S3 showed that the spiked recoveries of MG in water samples ranged from 95.6% to 101.5% with RSD below 4.5%, and the recoveries in fish samples ranged from 96.4% to 102.2% with RSD below 4.6%, which were similar to the HPLC results. Thus, the above results indicated that this method established was reliable and accurate, which was suitable for the determination of MG in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual analysis of MG by N, P-CDs fluorescent hydrogel kit
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that on-site detection and visualization of MG is of great practical significance for ensuring environmental safety, food safety and human health. Consequently, this study prepared a novel fluorescent hydrogel kit based on the fluorescent characteristic of N, P-CDs for visual analysis of MG. As presented in Fig. 7 A, with the increasing of MG concentration in the range of 0–120 µmol·L−1, the orange fluorescence of the hydrogel exhibited a gradually shallow trend under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp. The reason why the red fluorescence N, P-CDs showed the orange fluorescence was that the low concentration of N, P-CDs was added to the hydrogels, and these hydrogels were irradiated at 365 nm UV light instead of the optimal excitation wavelength of 611 nm. Subsequently, some potential interfering substances were also added to the prepared hydrogels to assess the selectivity of this method by recording the changes in fluorescence. It could be seen from Fig. 7B that the hydrogel with MG displayed obvious fluorescence quenching, while the addition of other substances hardly affected the fluorescence of the hydrogels, which revealed that this method showed excellent selectivity for MG. Therefore, the low cost and facile N, P-CDs fluorescent hydrogel kit possesses great application prospects for on-site detection and visual semi-quantitative analysis of MG.

In addition, this study also evaluated the cytotoxicity of N, P-CDs by calculating the survival rate of three kinds of cells using the CCK-8 method. As shown in Fig. S5, after the MEF, HCT-116 and Hepa 1–6 cells were incubated with different concentrations of N, P-CDs for 36 h, the survival rate of MEF cells was higher than 85%, and the survival rates of HCT-116 and Hepa 1–6 cells were above 75%. The above results indicate that the prepared N, P-CDs have low cytotoxicity, which provides a guarantee for potential applications in the biomedical field.

In conclusion, a pH-dependent red fluorescent N, P-CDs with low toxicity was synthesized by simple hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid as precursors, and a facile, sensitive and rapid method for the determination of pH and MG was established. Under acidic conditions, the fluorescence of N, P-CDs decreased due to the reduction of protonation of nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of N, P-CDs, and exhibited a good linear relationship with pH in the range of 3.8–5.6. And the fluorescence of N, P-CDs was quenched by MG through the IFE mechanism. Simultaneously, this fluorescent probe showed high sensitivity for MG with LODs of 0.07 and 0.74 µmol·L−1, and has been applied to the detection of MG in water and fish samples with satisfactory recoveries ranging from 95.6% to 102.2%. Furthermore, a kind of low cost, high efficiency and simple N, P-CDs fluorescent hydrogel kit was successfully prepared for the visual detection of MG. Hence, the simple, efficient and accurate method established in this study provides a new idea for monitoring MG in water environment and fish products.

Ning Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jianqiang Song: Software, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Heng Ye: Apparatus, Software. Longshan Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020-181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2022.112985.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unary and binary adsorption studies of lead and malachite green onto a nanomagnetic copper ferrite/drumstick pod biomass composite
